content: a thing that I've seen come up in languages with enumerations is wanting to get a list of all the different values in the enumeration so you can, for example, make a dropdown out of them. So in Roc it might be like Color : [ Red, Green, Yellow, Blue, Purple ] and then I want to get a List Color which has one element for each of the different tags in the tag union
I think it's worth Roc having a language feature like this; the question is how it should look/work

here's one idea:

Color : [ Red, Green, Yellow, Blue, Purple ]

allColors : List Color
allColors = ...Color

so, ... in front of a type means "if this is a closed tag union with no payloads, give me a List consisting of one of each of its elements"

it's pretty specific syntax, but I haven't seen a nice alternative that keeps the type and the list in sync - there's a technique you can use to get a compiler error if add a new element to the list and forget to remove an old one, but it's be easy to make a copy/paste error when constructing or modifying the when, which would mean they'd be out of sync and the compiler wouldn't be able to help
the reasoning behind ... in particular is wanting to use something that won't interfere with autocomplete
the order is definition order?

for example, if it was like Color.* then whenever you type Color. in the editor, if there's both a Color module and a Color type in scope (likely!) then one of the autocomplete suggestions will probably be Color.* even though that's rarely what you want
hm, good question
that allows control, at least
but it has the downside that now you can cause bugs by reordering those
whereas otherwise it's safe to order them however you want
right
Supporting open tag unions too would be nice
I think I'd probably say sort them alphabetically

and then if you want an ordered one, you can go like 

Color : [ Red, Green, Yellow, Blue, Purple ]

allColors : List Color
allColors = ...Color

orderedColors = [ Green, Red, Yellow, Blue, Purple ]

expect List.len orderedColors == List.len allColors
so the expect will tell you if you ever add or remove one and forget to update the ordered one
you could also go further and do like a List.containsAll (or whatever) to make sure they have the same contents

Supporting open tag unions too would be nice

I was thinking for that you could do like ...(Color []) to close it (assuming you have Color *)
because there's no such thing as an open list, so at some point it's got to decide on exactly what the elements are :big_smile:
and that's what a closed union would be
As long as closed unions can't be inferred, I expect that syntax will come up a lot
maybe, although the use cases where I've run into it in the past have all been (or would have been the equivalent of) closed unions...guess we'll see!

Richard Feldman said:

I think I'd probably say sort them alphabetically

I think sorting by definition order would be less error prone. If I do a rename I’m probably not thinking about how that might reorder a list elsewhere. Alternatively what if there was no syntax for enumerating all tags, but there was a containsAllTags function? You could use that with expect to catch yourself when you forget to add a new tag. This has the advantage of not needing new syntax and makes sure the user is explicit with their tag ordering.
what type would this function have? crazy idea, does hasAllTags : List ([]a) -> Bool work?
If hasAllTags : List ([]a) -> Bool is possible then that makes sense. Otherwise hasAllTags : List a -> Bool and if the user passes in a List Int or List String then it always returns False.
interestingly, List.hasAllTags : List []* -> Bool works with tags that have payloads too, which is potentially useful
very cool!

so a concrete example would be:

Color : [ R, G, B ]

colors : List Color
colors = [ G, R, B ]

expect List.hasAllTags colors
also would naturally work for open tag unions
so I think that covers the "what if I add a new tag to Color and forget to update my dropdown" case

if I remove a tag from Color and forget to update my dropdown, List.hasAllTags will still return the same answer, but as long as I have a type annotation on the contents of the dropdown (saying that it's a List Color) then I'll get a type mismatch
for open tag unions the return value is always false?

so the technique could be:

  1. Make sure to give your list an explicit type annotation
  2. Use expect List.hasAllTags on it

for open tag unions the return value is always false?

that's certainly an intuitive way for it to work, based on the name! :big_smile:
since it'd have to be an infinitely long list to have all the tags in an open union
although it'd probably be more useful if it just returned whether all the tags mentioned in the open union were present :stuck_out_tongue:
although it's pretty easy to close it with a type annotation
which you could even do with a def inside the expect if need be

I agree with the point that developers don't want to have a list of constructors, they mostly want a guarantee that their literals have this property that they're all there. And I also think that providing a list will not always be what/how the user wants it.

If this expect can give the guarantee through static analysis at compile time or very close to it and without runtime cost, then that's a nice solution :+1:
